text: We demonstrate for the first time the topotactic oxidative insertion of weakly coordinating counter-ions (SCI) like BF4− and PF6− into the redox-active metal organic framework (MOF) Fe2(dobpdc) (dobpdc4− = 4,4′-dioxidobiphenyl-3,3′-dicarboxylate) as well as the electrochemical properties that arise from this process. Insertion of both SCI into Fe2(dobpdc) leads only to a slight shrinkage of the MOF scaffold and in situ UV−vis spectroscopy reveals a broad intervalence charge transfer band, indicative of better charge delocalization into the framework. Our study shows that the metal centers as well as the MOF linkers can be oxidized but the reduction of the metal centers is fully reversible and both reduction and oxidation take place in a stoichiometric manner under both chemical and electrochemical conditions. These features are governed by nanoconfinement effects and vary strongly with counter-ion size and solvent properties and type. On account of reversible anion insertion properties into Fe2(dobpdc), we exemplified the utilization of this redox-active MOF material as cathode in a dual-ion battery in which a sodium-metal anode is employed. The novel cathode shows a remarkably high initial reduction potential while cycling could be achieved over at least 50 charge/discharge cycles and the material reaches a specific energy of 316 Wh/kg.
